primitives.name: Vector Definition
primitives.formalStatement: A vector v in R^n is an ordered n-tuple v = (v_1, v_2, ..., v_n) where each v_i is a real number. Vectors represent both magnitude and direction in n-dimensional space.
primitives.computationalForm: Represent as an array of n real numbers. Access component i as v[i]. Display as column or row notation.

dependencies.description: Vectors are elements of a vector space defined by these axioms

compositionRules.yields: Vector arithmetic in R^n
conditions: Vectors must be in the same R^n space
compositionRules.example: v = (1,2,3) and w = (4,5,6) compose via addition to give v+w = (5,7,9)

applicabilityPatterns: I need to represent a quantity with both magnitude and direction
How do I work with points or directions in multiple dimensions
Describe a displacement or velocity in n-dimensional space
buildLabs: Build Lab 11.1: Visualizing Vectors in R^2 and R^3

primitives.name: Vector Addition and Scalar Multiplication
primitives.formalStatement: For vectors u, v in R^n and scalar c in R: (u+v)_i = u_i + v_i (componentwise addition) and (cv)_i = c * v_i (scalar multiplication). These operations satisfy closure, commutativity, associativity, and distributivity.
primitives.computationalForm: Add vectors componentwise: (u+v)[i] = u[i] + v[i]. Scale by multiplying each component: (cv)[i] = c * v[i].

dependencies.description: Operations are defined on vectors

compositionRules.yields: Complete vector arithmetic system

applicabilityPatterns: How do I add or scale vectors
Combine forces or velocities acting on an object
Compute a linear combination of vectors

primitives.name: Vector Space Axioms
primitives.formalStatement: A vector space V over a field F is a set with two operations (addition, scalar multiplication) satisfying 8 axioms: closure under addition and scalar multiplication, commutativity and associativity of addition, existence of zero vector and additive inverses, and distributive laws connecting addition with scalar multiplication.
primitives.computationalForm: To verify a set is a vector space: check all 8 axioms. 1) u+v in V, 2) cv in V, 3) u+v=v+u, 4) (u+v)+w=u+(v+w), 5) exists 0: v+0=v, 6) exists -v: v+(-v)=0, 7) c(u+v)=cu+cv, 8) (c+d)v=cv+dv, plus 1*v=v and c(dv)=(cd)v.

compositionRules.yields: Theory of linear maps between vector spaces
conditions: Both domain and codomain are vector spaces
compositionRules.example: R^2 and R^3 are vector spaces; T: R^2 -> R^3 is a linear transformation between them

applicabilityPatterns: Is this set a vector space
Verify the axioms for a proposed vector space structure
What properties must a space have to support linear algebra

primitives.name: Dot Product (Inner Product)
primitives.formalStatement: The dot product of u, v in R^n is u . v = sum_{i=1}^{n} u_i * v_i. Geometrically, u . v = ||u|| ||v|| cos(theta) where theta is the angle between u and v.
primitives.computationalForm: Compute sum of products of corresponding components: u.v = u[1]*v[1] + u[2]*v[2] + ... + u[n]*v[n]. Result is a scalar.

dependencies.description: Dot product is defined on vectors

compositionRules.yields: Angle measurement between vectors: cos(theta) = (u.v)/(||u|| ||v||)
compositionRules.example: Angle between (1,0) and (1,1): cos(theta) = 1/sqrt(2), so theta = pi/4

applicabilityPatterns: Find the angle between two vectors
Compute the projection of one vector onto another
Check if two vectors are perpendicular
buildLabs: Build Lab 11.2: Dot Products and Angles

primitives.name: Cross Product
primitives.formalStatement: The cross product of u, v in R^3 is u x v = (u_2*v_3 - u_3*v_2, u_3*v_1 - u_1*v_3, u_1*v_2 - u_2*v_1). The result is a vector perpendicular to both u and v with magnitude ||u|| ||v|| sin(theta).
primitives.computationalForm: Compute via determinant expansion: u x v = det([i,j,k; u_1,u_2,u_3; v_1,v_2,v_3]). Result is a vector in R^3.

dependencies.description: Cross product operates on 3D vectors

compositionRules.yields: Scalar triple product: u . (v x w) = volume of parallelepiped
compositionRules.example: Volume of parallelepiped spanned by (1,0,0), (0,1,0), (0,0,1) is |1.(0x0 - 0x0, 0x1 - 1x0, 1x0 - 0x0)| = 1

applicabilityPatterns: Find a vector perpendicular to two given vectors
Compute the area of a parallelogram spanned by two vectors
Determine the orientation of three vectors in 3D space
buildLabs: Build Lab 11.3: Cross Products and Surfaces

primitives.name: Vector Norm (Magnitude)
primitives.formalStatement: The Euclidean norm of v in R^n is ||v|| = sqrt(v . v) = sqrt(sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i^2). It measures the length (magnitude) of the vector. A unit vector has ||v|| = 1.
primitives.computationalForm: Compute ||v|| = sqrt(v[1]^2 + v[2]^2 + ... + v[n]^2). Normalize: v_hat = v / ||v||.

dependencies.description: Norm is defined via the dot product: ||v|| = sqrt(v.v)

compositionRules.yields: Distance metric in R^n: d(u,v) = ||u-v||
compositionRules.example: Distance from (1,2) to (4,6) is ||(3,4)|| = 5

applicabilityPatterns: Find the length or magnitude of a vector
Normalize a vector to unit length
Compute distance between two points in R^n

primitives.name: Orthogonality and Projection
primitives.formalStatement: Vectors u and v are orthogonal iff u . v = 0. The orthogonal projection of u onto v is proj_v(u) = (u . v / v . v) * v. The component of u orthogonal to v is u - proj_v(u).
primitives.computationalForm: Test orthogonality: compute u.v, check if zero. Project: proj = (u.v / v.v) * v. Orthogonal decomposition: u = proj_v(u) + (u - proj_v(u)).

dependencies.description: Orthogonality is defined via the dot product being zero
Projection formula uses norms

compositionRules.yields: Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization: convert any basis to orthogonal basis
conditions: Set of linearly independent vectors given
compositionRules.example: Given {(1,1), (1,0)}, Gram-Schmidt produces {(1,1), (1/2,-1/2)} (orthogonal)

applicabilityPatterns: Check if two vectors are perpendicular
Decompose a vector into components along and perpendicular to a direction
Find the closest point on a line or subspace to a given point

primitives.name: Linear Independence
primitives.formalStatement: Vectors v_1, ..., v_k in V are linearly independent if the only solution to c_1*v_1 + ... + c_k*v_k = 0 is c_1 = ... = c_k = 0. Otherwise they are linearly dependent.
primitives.computationalForm: Set up homogeneous system [v_1 | ... | v_k] c = 0 and row reduce. If only trivial solution exists, vectors are independent. Equivalently, check if the matrix has full column rank.

dependencies.description: Linear independence is defined via linear combinations

compositionRules.yields: Dimension of a vector space: size of any basis
compositionRules.example: Standard basis {e_1,...,e_n} in R^n has n linearly independent vectors, so dim(R^n) = n

applicabilityPatterns: Determine if a set of vectors can represent all combinations uniquely
Check if one vector is redundant given others
Find the minimum number of vectors needed to span a space

primitives.name: Span and Basis
primitives.formalStatement: The span of vectors {v_1,...,v_k} is the set of all linear combinations: span(S) = {c_1*v_1 + ... + c_k*v_k : c_i in F}. A basis of V is a linearly independent set that spans V. The dimension dim(V) is the number of basis vectors.
primitives.computationalForm: To find a basis: place vectors as columns, row reduce, take columns corresponding to pivot positions. dim(V) = number of pivots.

dependencies.description: Basis requires linear independence
Span requires linear combinations

compositionRules.yields: Matrix representation of a linear map with respect to chosen bases
conditions: Bases chosen for domain and codomain
compositionRules.example: Rotation by theta in R^2 relative to standard basis gives matrix [[cos(theta), -sin(theta)],[sin(theta), cos(theta)]]

applicabilityPatterns: Find a minimal set of vectors that generates a subspace
Determine the dimension of a vector space or subspace
Express any vector as coordinates relative to a basis

primitives.name: Triangle Inequality for Vectors
primitives.formalStatement: For any vectors u, v in R^n: ||u + v|| <= ||u|| + ||v||. Equality holds iff u and v point in the same direction (one is a nonnegative scalar multiple of the other).
primitives.computationalForm: Verify by computing both sides. Proof uses Cauchy-Schwarz: ||u+v||^2 = ||u||^2 + 2(u.v) + ||v||^2 <= ||u||^2 + 2||u||||v|| + ||v||^2 = (||u|| + ||v||)^2.

dependencies.description: Triangle inequality is a property of the norm
Proof depends on Cauchy-Schwarz

compositionRules.yields: Metric space structure on R^n with triangle inequality as key axiom

applicabilityPatterns: Bound the length of a sum of vectors
Prove that a norm defines a valid metric
Estimate distances using the triangle inequality

primitives.name: Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality
primitives.formalStatement: |u . v| <= ||u|| * ||v|| for all u, v in an inner product space. Equality holds iff u and v are linearly dependent (one is a scalar multiple of the other).
primitives.computationalForm: Verify: compute |u.v| and ||u||*||v|| separately. Proof: consider ||u - (u.v/v.v)v||^2 >= 0, expand to get the inequality.

dependencies.description: Inequality involves the inner product
Inequality involves the norm

compositionRules.yields: Angle between vectors is well-defined: cos(theta) = u.v/(||u||||v||) is in [-1,1]
conditions: Nonzero vectors in inner product space

applicabilityPatterns: Prove that the angle between vectors is well-defined
Bound a dot product in terms of norms
Establish inequalities in inner product spaces

primitives.name: Subspace
primitives.formalStatement: A subspace W of a vector space V is a nonempty subset of V that is closed under addition and scalar multiplication: (1) 0 in W, (2) u,v in W implies u+v in W, (3) u in W, c in F implies cu in W.
primitives.computationalForm: To verify W is a subspace: check three conditions. Common subspaces: null space of a matrix, column space, row space, eigenspaces.

dependencies.description: Subspace inherits vector space structure

compositionRules.yields: Kernel and image of a linear map are subspaces
compositionRules.example: Kernel of T(x,y,z)=(x+y, 2z) is the subspace {(x,-x,0)} of R^3

applicabilityPatterns: Is this subset of a vector space also a vector space
Find the null space or column space of a matrix
Identify invariant subspaces of a transformation

primitives.name: Matrix Definition and Operations
primitives.formalStatement: An m x n matrix A is a rectangular array of scalars with m rows and n columns: A = [a_{ij}] where 1 <= i <= m, 1 <= j <= n. Matrix addition is componentwise; scalar multiplication scales all entries.
primitives.computationalForm: Store as 2D array. Addition: C[i][j] = A[i][j] + B[i][j]. Scalar multiplication: (cA)[i][j] = c * A[i][j]. Requires matching dimensions for addition.

dependencies.description: Matrices represent linear maps between spaces with chosen bases

compositionRules.yields: Matrix algebra: a ring of n x n matrices
compositionRules.example: 2x2 matrices with addition and multiplication form a ring (non-commutative)

applicabilityPatterns: Represent a linear system of equations in compact form
Store and manipulate tabular numerical data
Encode a linear transformation as a matrix

primitives.name: Linear Transformation
primitives.formalStatement: A function T: V -> W between vector spaces is a linear transformation if T(u+v) = T(u)+T(v) and T(cv) = cT(v) for all u,v in V and scalars c. Equivalently, T(c_1*v_1 + c_2*v_2) = c_1*T(v_1) + c_2*T(v_2).
primitives.computationalForm: Represent T as a matrix A: T(v) = Av. Find matrix by computing T on each basis vector. Composition T2 o T1 corresponds to matrix product A2 * A1.

dependencies.description: Linear transformations are maps between vector spaces
Matrix representation requires choosing bases

compositionRules.yields: Concrete matrix representation of abstract linear maps
compositionRules.example: Differentiation d/dx on polynomials of degree <= 2: matrix [[0,1,0],[0,0,2],[0,0,0]] relative to basis {1, x, x^2}

applicabilityPatterns: Define a function that preserves vector space structure
Represent a geometric transformation as a matrix
Map between different vector spaces while preserving linearity
buildLabs: Build Lab 12.2: Geometric Transformations as Matrices

primitives.name: Matrix Multiplication as Composition
primitives.formalStatement: For an m x n matrix A and n x p matrix B, the product AB is the m x p matrix with (AB)_{ij} = sum_{k=1}^{n} a_{ik} * b_{kj}. Matrix multiplication corresponds to composition of linear transformations: [T2 o T1] = [T2][T1].
primitives.computationalForm: Compute (AB)[i][j] = dot product of row i of A with column j of B. Complexity: O(mnp) for m x n times n x p. Note: AB != BA in general.

dependencies.description: Multiplication is defined on matrices
Multiplication encodes composition of linear maps

compositionRules.yields: Composition of transformations as a single matrix
conditions: Dimensions are compatible: A is m x n, B is n x p
compositionRules.example: Rotation by 30 degrees then 45 degrees: R(45)*R(30) = R(75)

applicabilityPatterns: Combine two linear transformations into one
Multiply matrices to solve a system or transform data
Apply a sequence of operations as a single matrix product

primitives.name: Determinant
primitives.formalStatement: The determinant of a square matrix A is det(A) = sum over permutations sigma of sign(sigma) * product_{i=1}^{n} a_{i,sigma(i)}. For 2x2: det([[a,b],[c,d]]) = ad - bc. det(A) = 0 iff A is singular.
primitives.computationalForm: For 2x2: ad-bc. For 3x3: cofactor expansion along any row or column. For n x n: LU decomposition and multiply diagonal entries. det(AB) = det(A)*det(B).

dependencies.description: Determinant is defined on square matrices

compositionRules.yields: det(A) = product of eigenvalues; characteristic polynomial via det(A - lambda*I) = 0
compositionRules.example: For A = [[2,1],[0,3]], det(A) = 6 = 2*3, eigenvalues are 2 and 3

applicabilityPatterns: Check if a matrix is invertible
Compute the volume scaling factor of a linear transformation
Find eigenvalues via the characteristic polynomial

primitives.name: Rank-Nullity Theorem
primitives.formalStatement: For a linear transformation T: V -> W where V is finite-dimensional: dim(V) = rank(T) + nullity(T), where rank(T) = dim(im(T)) and nullity(T) = dim(ker(T)).
primitives.computationalForm: Row reduce the matrix of T. rank = number of pivots. nullity = number of free variables. Verify: rank + nullity = number of columns.

dependencies.description: Theorem is about linear transformations
Kernel and image are subspaces
Rank and nullity measure dimensions of subspaces

compositionRules.yields: Complete solution of linear systems: particular + null space
compositionRules.example: For 3x4 matrix with rank 2: nullity = 2, so solution set is 2-dimensional affine subspace

applicabilityPatterns: Relate the dimensions of the kernel and image of a linear map
Determine how many free variables a linear system has
Characterize the solution space of a homogeneous system

primitives.name: Eigenvalue and Eigenvector
primitives.formalStatement: A scalar lambda is an eigenvalue of a square matrix A if there exists a nonzero vector v such that Av = lambda*v. The vector v is the corresponding eigenvector. The set of all eigenvectors for lambda (plus 0) is the eigenspace E_lambda = ker(A - lambda*I).
primitives.computationalForm: Solve det(A - lambda*I) = 0 to find eigenvalues. For each lambda, solve (A - lambda*I)v = 0 to find eigenvectors. The eigenspace is the null space of A - lambda*I.

dependencies.description: Eigenvalue equation Av = lambda*v uses matrix-vector product
Finding eigenvalues requires solving det(A - lambda*I) = 0

compositionRules.yields: A = PDP^{-1} where D is diagonal of eigenvalues and P is matrix of eigenvectors
conditions: Matrix has n linearly independent eigenvectors
compositionRules.example: A = [[2,1],[0,3]] has eigenvalues 2,3 and eigenvectors (1,0),(1,1) => A = PDP^{-1}

applicabilityPatterns: Find the directions that a transformation merely scales
Analyze the long-term behavior of a dynamical system
Diagonalize a matrix for easier computation
buildLabs: Build Lab 12.3: Eigenvalues and Geometric Interpretation

primitives.name: Characteristic Polynomial
primitives.formalStatement: The characteristic polynomial of an n x n matrix A is p(lambda) = det(A - lambda*I), a degree-n polynomial in lambda. Its roots are the eigenvalues of A. By the fundamental theorem of algebra, A has exactly n eigenvalues (counting multiplicity) in C.
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Form A - lambda*I. 2. Compute det(A - lambda*I) by cofactor expansion or row reduction. 3. Factor the resulting polynomial. 4. Roots are eigenvalues.

dependencies.description: Characteristic polynomial is the tool for finding eigenvalues
Defined as determinant of A - lambda*I

compositionRules.yields: Complete eigenvalue computation pipeline
compositionRules.example: For A = [[1,2],[3,4]]: p(lambda) = (1-lambda)(4-lambda)-6 = lambda^2-5*lambda-2, roots: (5+/-sqrt(33))/2

applicabilityPatterns: Find all eigenvalues of a matrix systematically
Determine the algebraic multiplicity of each eigenvalue
Compute a polynomial whose roots encode matrix behavior

primitives.name: Diagonalization
primitives.formalStatement: An n x n matrix A is diagonalizable if A = PDP^{-1} where D = diag(lambda_1,...,lambda_n) and P = [v_1 | ... | v_n] with v_i the eigenvectors. A is diagonalizable iff it has n linearly independent eigenvectors.
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Find eigenvalues from characteristic polynomial. 2. Find eigenvectors for each eigenvalue. 3. If n independent eigenvectors exist, form P and D. 4. Verify AP = PD. Powers: A^k = PD^kP^{-1}.

dependencies.description: Diagonalization requires eigenvalues and eigenvectors
A = PDP^{-1} requires inverting P

compositionRules.yields: Efficient matrix powers: A^k = PD^kP^{-1} with D^k trivial
compositionRules.example: If A has eigenvalues 2,3 then A^{100} = P*diag(2^{100},3^{100})*P^{-1}

applicabilityPatterns: Simplify repeated application of a linear transformation
Compute large powers of a matrix efficiently
Solve systems of differential equations via diagonalization
buildLabs: Build Lab 12.4: Diagonalization and Matrix Powers

primitives.name: Singular Value Decomposition
primitives.formalStatement: Every m x n matrix A has a decomposition A = U * Sigma * V^T where U is m x m orthogonal, V is n x n orthogonal, and Sigma is m x n diagonal with nonneg entries sigma_1 >= sigma_2 >= ... >= 0 (the singular values).
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Compute eigenvalues of A^T*A (these are sigma_i^2). 2. sigma_i = sqrt(eigenvalue_i). 3. V = eigenvectors of A^T*A. 4. U = eigenvectors of A*A^T. 5. Sigma = diag(sigma_1,...). Low-rank approximation: keep top k singular values.

dependencies.description: SVD is based on eigendecomposition of A^T*A and A*A^T
U and V are orthogonal matrices

compositionRules.yields: Pseudoinverse via SVD: A^+ = V * Sigma^+ * U^T for least squares solutions
conditions: A is any m x n matrix
compositionRules.example: For overdetermined system Ax = b, pseudoinverse gives x = A^+*b = V*Sigma^+*U^T*b

applicabilityPatterns: Decompose any matrix into rotation, scaling, and rotation components
Find the best low-rank approximation to a matrix
Compute the pseudoinverse for least-squares problems
buildLabs: Build Lab 12.5: SVD and Data Compression

primitives.name: Gaussian Elimination
primitives.formalStatement: Gaussian elimination transforms a matrix to row echelon form via elementary row operations: (1) swap two rows, (2) multiply a row by a nonzero scalar, (3) add a scalar multiple of one row to another. Back substitution then solves the system.
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Find leftmost nonzero column (pivot column). 2. Swap to bring nonzero entry to pivot position. 3. Eliminate all entries below pivot using row operations. 4. Repeat for next row/column. 5. Back-substitute from bottom up.

dependencies.description: Algorithm operates on matrices

compositionRules.yields: Complete linear system solver: reduces Ax = b to triangular form
conditions: Any m x n matrix augmented with b

applicabilityPatterns: Solve a system of linear equations
Find the rank of a matrix
Determine if a system has a unique solution, infinitely many, or none

primitives.name: Matrix Inverse
primitives.formalStatement: The inverse of an n x n matrix A is the unique matrix A^{-1} satisfying A*A^{-1} = A^{-1}*A = I. A is invertible iff det(A) != 0 iff rank(A) = n iff all eigenvalues are nonzero.
primitives.computationalForm: Method 1: Augment [A | I] and row reduce to [I | A^{-1}]. Method 2: A^{-1} = (1/det(A)) * adj(A). For 2x2: [[a,b],[c,d]]^{-1} = (1/(ad-bc))*[[d,-b],[-c,a]].

dependencies.description: Row reduction is the standard algorithm for computing inverses
Invertibility requires nonzero determinant

compositionRules.yields: Invertible transformations: bijective linear maps
compositionRules.example: Rotation inverse: R(theta)^{-1} = R(-theta)

applicabilityPatterns: Solve a linear system Ax = b by computing x = A^{-1}b
Undo a linear transformation
Check if a transformation is bijective

primitives.name: Orthogonal and Unitary Matrices
primitives.formalStatement: A real square matrix Q is orthogonal if Q^T*Q = Q*Q^T = I, equivalently Q^{-1} = Q^T. Its columns form an orthonormal basis. A complex matrix U is unitary if U^{dagger}*U = I. Orthogonal matrices preserve lengths and angles: ||Qv|| = ||v||.
primitives.computationalForm: Check: Q^T * Q should equal I. Properties: det(Q) = +/-1, eigenvalues have |lambda| = 1. Examples: rotation matrices, reflection matrices, permutation matrices.

dependencies.description: Columns of orthogonal matrix are orthonormal
Defined via matrix product Q^T*Q = I

compositionRules.yields: Spectral decomposition of symmetric matrices: A = Q*D*Q^T
compositionRules.example: Symmetric 2x2 matrix decomposes with orthogonal eigenvector matrix

applicabilityPatterns: Find a transformation that preserves distances and angles
Represent rotations and reflections as matrices
Construct an orthonormal basis for a subspace

primitives.name: Spectral Theorem
primitives.formalStatement: Every real symmetric matrix A has an orthogonal diagonalization: A = Q*D*Q^T where Q is orthogonal and D is diagonal with real eigenvalues. Every Hermitian matrix has a unitary diagonalization. In particular, all eigenvalues of a symmetric/Hermitian matrix are real.
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Verify A = A^T (symmetric). 2. Find eigenvalues (guaranteed real). 3. Find orthonormal eigenvectors (Gram-Schmidt if needed). 4. Form Q = [v_1|...|v_n], D = diag(lambda_1,...,lambda_n). Then A = QDQ^T.

dependencies.description: Spectral theorem is a special case of diagonalization for symmetric matrices
The change-of-basis matrix is orthogonal

compositionRules.yields: Connection between spectral theorem and SVD: for symmetric A, SVD reduces to spectral decomposition
compositionRules.example: For symmetric A: A^T*A = A^2, so singular values are |eigenvalues|, and U = V (up to signs)

applicabilityPatterns: Diagonalize a symmetric or Hermitian matrix with an orthogonal basis
Prove that all eigenvalues of a symmetric matrix are real
Decompose a quadratic form into principal axes

primitives.name: Change of Basis
primitives.formalStatement: If B = {b_1,...,b_n} and B' = {b'_1,...,b'_n} are two bases of V, the change-of-basis matrix P from B to B' satisfies [v]_{B'} = P^{-1}[v]_B. The matrix of T in basis B' is [T]_{B'} = P^{-1}[T]_B P.
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Express each b_i as a linear combination of B' vectors. 2. Form P with these coefficients as columns. 3. New coordinates: [v]_{B'} = P^{-1}[v]_B. 4. New matrix: [T]_{B'} = P^{-1}[T]_B*P.

dependencies.description: Change of basis operates between two bases
Change-of-basis matrix must be invertible

compositionRules.yields: Diagonalization is change of basis to eigenbasis: A = PDP^{-1}
compositionRules.example: Choosing eigenvectors as new basis converts A to diagonal form

applicabilityPatterns: Express a vector or transformation in a different coordinate system
Simplify a matrix by choosing a convenient basis
Convert between different representations of the same linear map

primitives.name: Least Squares
primitives.formalStatement: For an overdetermined system Ax = b (more equations than unknowns), the least squares solution minimizes ||Ax - b||^2. It satisfies the normal equations A^T*A*x = A^T*b. If A has full column rank, x = (A^T*A)^{-1}*A^T*b.
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Form normal equations: A^T*A*x = A^T*b. 2. Solve (A^T*A is square and invertible if A has full rank). Alternative: use SVD pseudoinverse x = A^+*b.

dependencies.description: Least squares finds the orthogonal projection of b onto column space of A
Full rank condition determines unique solution existence

compositionRules.yields: Numerically stable least squares via SVD pseudoinverse
conditions: Any m x n matrix A with m > n
compositionRules.example: Fitting a line y = mx + c to n data points: A = [[x_1,1],...,[x_n,1]], b = [y_1,...,y_n]

applicabilityPatterns: Find the best approximate solution to an overdetermined system
Fit a model to data with more observations than parameters
Perform linear regression
buildLabs: Build Lab 12.6: Linear Regression via Least Squares

primitives.name: Gradient
primitives.formalStatement: The gradient of a scalar field f: R^n -> R is the vector of partial derivatives: grad(f) = nabla f = (df/dx_1, df/dx_2, ..., df/dx_n). It points in the direction of steepest ascent and its magnitude is the rate of maximum increase.
primitives.computationalForm: Compute each partial derivative df/dx_i holding other variables constant. Assemble into vector. At point p: direction of steepest ascent is nabla f(p) / ||nabla f(p)||.

dependencies.description: Gradient is a vector

compositionRules.yields: Fundamental theorem for line integrals: integral of grad(f) . dr = f(b) - f(a)
compositionRules.example: For f(x,y) = x^2+y^2, integral of nabla f from (0,0) to (1,1) = f(1,1)-f(0,0) = 2

applicabilityPatterns: Find the direction of steepest increase of a function
Compute the rate of change in an arbitrary direction via directional derivative
Set up optimization conditions: critical points where nabla f = 0
buildLabs: Build Lab 13.1: Gradient Fields and Level Curves

primitives.name: Divergence
primitives.formalStatement: The divergence of a vector field F = (F_1, F_2, F_3): R^3 -> R^3 is the scalar field div(F) = nabla . F = dF_1/dx + dF_2/dy + dF_3/dz. It measures the rate at which the field expands or compresses at each point.
primitives.computationalForm: Sum the partial derivatives of each component with respect to its corresponding variable: div(F) = dF_1/dx_1 + dF_2/dx_2 + ... + dF_n/dx_n.

dependencies.description: Divergence uses the same nabla operator as gradient but as dot product

compositionRules.yields: Laplacian: div(grad(f)) = nabla^2 f = sum of second partials
compositionRules.example: For f(x,y) = x^2 + y^2: grad(f) = (2x, 2y), div(grad(f)) = 2 + 2 = 4

applicabilityPatterns: Determine if a vector field is expanding or compressing at a point
Check if a fluid flow is incompressible (div = 0)
Compute the source/sink strength of a field

primitives.name: Curl
primitives.formalStatement: The curl of a vector field F = (F_1, F_2, F_3) is curl(F) = nabla x F = (dF_3/dy - dF_2/dz, dF_1/dz - dF_3/dx, dF_2/dx - dF_1/dy). It measures the rotational tendency of the field at each point.
primitives.computationalForm: Compute as formal determinant of [i,j,k; d/dx,d/dy,d/dz; F_1,F_2,F_3]. Each component is a difference of cross-partial derivatives.

dependencies.description: Curl uses the nabla operator as cross product
Curl is formally nabla cross F

compositionRules.yields: Identity: curl(grad(f)) = 0 for any twice-differentiable scalar field
compositionRules.example: For f(x,y,z) = xyz: grad = (yz, xz, xy), curl of this = (x-x, y-y, z-z) = (0,0,0)

applicabilityPatterns: Determine if a vector field has rotational behavior
Check if a field is conservative (curl = 0)
Compute circulation density around a point

primitives.name: Line Integral
primitives.formalStatement: The line integral of a vector field F along a curve C parametrized by r(t), a <= t <= b, is integral_C F . dr = integral_a^b F(r(t)) . r'(t) dt. For a scalar field f: integral_C f ds = integral_a^b f(r(t)) ||r'(t)|| dt.
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Parametrize the curve: r(t). 2. Compute r'(t). 3. Evaluate F(r(t)). 4. Compute dot product F(r(t)) . r'(t). 5. Integrate from a to b.

dependencies.description: Line integral of vector field uses dot product F . dr
Defined for vector fields along curves

compositionRules.yields: Path-independent integral: integral depends only on endpoints if F = grad(f)
compositionRules.example: Work done by gravity: integral_C mg . dr = mgh depends only on height change

applicabilityPatterns: Compute work done by a force along a path
Calculate circulation of a vector field around a curve
Evaluate integral of a function along a curve
buildLabs: Build Lab 13.2: Work and Line Integrals

primitives.name: Surface Integral
primitives.formalStatement: The surface integral of F over S parametrized by r(u,v) is integral_S F . dS = integral_D F(r(u,v)) . (r_u x r_v) du dv. For a scalar: integral_S f dS = integral_D f(r(u,v)) ||r_u x r_v|| du dv.
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Parametrize surface: r(u,v). 2. Compute r_u and r_v (partial derivatives). 3. Normal: n = r_u x r_v. 4. Evaluate F . n at each point. 5. Integrate over parameter domain D.

dependencies.description: Surface element dS involves cross product of tangent vectors
Surface integrals generalize line integrals to 2D

compositionRules.yields: Convert volume integrals to surface integrals and vice versa
compositionRules.example: Flux through sphere = integral of divergence over ball interior

applicabilityPatterns: Calculate flux of a vector field through a surface
Compute the total quantity passing through a boundary
Evaluate integrals over curved surfaces

primitives.name: Green's Theorem
primitives.formalStatement: For a positively oriented, piecewise smooth, simple closed curve C bounding a region D in R^2, and F = (P, Q) a C^1 vector field: oint_C P dx + Q dy = integral_D (dQ/dx - dP/dy) dA.
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Identify the boundary curve C and interior region D. 2. Compute dQ/dx - dP/dy. 3. Evaluate the double integral over D. This equals the line integral around C.

dependencies.description: Green's theorem relates line integrals to double integrals
The integrand dQ/dx - dP/dy is the 2D curl

compositionRules.yields: Area computation: A = (1/2) oint_C x dy - y dx
compositionRules.example: Area of circle: (1/2) oint (x dy - y dx) = pi*r^2 using parametrization x=r*cos(t), y=r*sin(t)

applicabilityPatterns: Convert a line integral around a closed curve to a double integral
Compute area enclosed by a curve using a line integral
Evaluate circulation of a 2D vector field
buildLabs: Build Lab 13.3: Green's Theorem and Area

primitives.name: Stokes' Theorem
primitives.formalStatement: For a smooth oriented surface S with boundary curve C (oriented consistently): integral_C F . dr = integral_S (curl F) . dS. The circulation of F around C equals the flux of curl(F) through S.
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Identify surface S and its boundary C. 2. Compute curl(F). 3. Choose: either evaluate line integral around C or surface integral of curl(F) over S, whichever is easier.

dependencies.description: Stokes' theorem involves curl of the vector field
Right side is a surface integral
Left side is a line integral
Green's theorem is the 2D case of Stokes' theorem

compositionRules.yields: For conservative F (curl=0), integral over any closed curve is 0
compositionRules.example: If F = grad(f), then curl(F) = 0, so oint_C F . dr = integral_S 0 dS = 0

applicabilityPatterns: Convert a line integral to a surface integral or vice versa
Relate circulation around a boundary to rotation through a surface
Prove that a conservative field has zero circulation

primitives.name: Divergence Theorem (Gauss's Theorem)
primitives.formalStatement: For a solid region E bounded by a closed surface S with outward normal: integral_S F . dS = integral_E div(F) dV. The total flux through S equals the integral of divergence over the enclosed volume.
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Identify closed surface S bounding volume E. 2. Compute div(F). 3. Choose: either compute surface integral directly or volume integral of divergence, whichever is simpler.

dependencies.description: Theorem involves divergence of the field
Left side is a surface integral

compositionRules.yields: Physical conservation laws: if div(F) = 0, total flux through any closed surface is 0
compositionRules.example: For incompressible fluid (div(v) = 0): flux into any volume = flux out

applicabilityPatterns: Convert a surface integral over a closed surface to a volume integral
Compute flux using divergence when surface integral is hard
Derive conservation laws in physics

primitives.name: Conservative Vector Field
primitives.formalStatement: A vector field F is conservative if F = grad(f) for some scalar potential f. Equivalently (in simply connected domains): curl(F) = 0, and the line integral of F is path-independent.
primitives.computationalForm: Test: compute curl(F). If curl(F) = 0 in a simply connected domain, F is conservative. Find potential: integrate F_1 w.r.t. x, then match remaining components.

dependencies.description: Conservative field is defined as gradient of a potential
Curl test determines if field is conservative

compositionRules.yields: Path independence: integral depends only on endpoints, not path
compositionRules.example: F = (2xy, x^2): potential f = x^2*y, integral from (0,0) to (1,1) = f(1,1) - f(0,0) = 1

applicabilityPatterns: Determine if a force field is conservative (has a potential)
Simplify line integral computation via path independence
Find a scalar potential function for a given vector field

primitives.name: Potential Function
primitives.formalStatement: For a conservative field F = (P, Q, R), the potential function f satisfies df/dx = P, df/dy = Q, df/dz = R. Recovery technique: integrate P w.r.t. x to get f = integral P dx + g(y,z), then determine g by matching df/dy = Q and df/dz = R.
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Integrate F_1 w.r.t. x_1: f = int F_1 dx_1 + g(x_2,...,x_n). 2. Differentiate f w.r.t. x_2 and set equal to F_2: solve for partial g. 3. Repeat for remaining variables. 4. Result: f such that grad(f) = F.

dependencies.description: Potential functions exist only for conservative fields

compositionRules.yields: Round-trip: f -> grad(f) = F -> recover f from F (up to constant)
compositionRules.example: F = (yz, xz, xy): f = xyz (check: grad(xyz) = (yz, xz, xy))

applicabilityPatterns: Find the potential energy function for a conservative force
Recover a scalar field whose gradient is a given vector field
Simplify work calculations by finding the potential

primitives.name: Jacobian Matrix
primitives.formalStatement: The Jacobian matrix of a function F: R^n -> R^m at point x is the m x n matrix J_F(x) with J_{ij} = dF_i/dx_j. It is the best linear approximation to F near x: F(x + h) approx F(x) + J_F(x)*h.
primitives.computationalForm: Compute all partial derivatives: J[i][j] = dF_i/dx_j. For coordinate transformations, |det(J)| is the volume scaling factor. Jacobian determinant used in change of variables for integration.

dependencies.description: Jacobian matrix generalizes the gradient to vector-valued functions
Jacobian is a matrix of partial derivatives

compositionRules.yields: Change of variables in integrals: dx dy = |det(J)| du dv
compositionRules.example: Polar coordinates: J = [[cos(t),-r*sin(t)],[sin(t),r*cos(t)]], |det(J)| = r, so dx dy = r dr dt

applicabilityPatterns: Linearize a nonlinear function at a point
Compute the volume scaling factor for a coordinate transformation
Apply the chain rule for multivariable functions

primitives.name: Chain Rule for Multivariable Functions
primitives.formalStatement: If g: R^m -> R^n and f: R^n -> R^p are differentiable, then the derivative of f o g at x is J_{fog}(x) = J_f(g(x)) * J_g(x). In the scalar case: d(f(g(t)))/dt = nabla f(g(t)) . g'(t).
primitives.computationalForm: Multiply Jacobian matrices: J of composition = J of outer evaluated at inner times J of inner. For scalar composition: df/dt = sum_i (df/dx_i)(dx_i/dt).

dependencies.description: Chain rule is expressed via Jacobian matrix multiplication
Composition of derivatives = product of Jacobians

compositionRules.yields: Directional derivative: D_u f = nabla f . u (chain rule along direction u)

applicabilityPatterns: Differentiate a composition of multivariable functions
Convert between different coordinate system derivatives
Compute how a function changes along a parametric path

primitives.name: Complex Function
primitives.formalStatement: A complex function f: C -> C maps complex numbers to complex numbers. Writing z = x + iy and f(z) = u(x,y) + iv(x,y), f decomposes into real and imaginary parts u, v: R^2 -> R. Complex functions can be visualized as transformations of the complex plane.
primitives.computationalForm: Decompose f(z) = u(x,y) + iv(x,y). Evaluate by substituting z = x + iy. Common functions: polynomials, exp(z) = e^x(cos y + i sin y), log(z) = ln|z| + i*arg(z).

dependencies.description: Complex functions can be viewed as R^2 -> R^2 vector fields

compositionRules.yields: Holomorphic function theory: differentiable complex functions have extraordinary properties
conditions: f is differentiable as a complex function
compositionRules.example: f(z) = z^2 is entire (holomorphic everywhere): f'(z) = 2z

applicabilityPatterns: Define and evaluate functions of a complex variable
Decompose a complex function into real and imaginary parts
Visualize how a function transforms the complex plane

primitives.name: Analyticity (Holomorphic Function)
primitives.formalStatement: A complex function f is analytic (holomorphic) at z_0 if f'(z_0) = lim_{h->0} (f(z_0+h)-f(z_0))/h exists, where h approaches 0 through complex values. f is entire if analytic on all of C. Analytic implies infinitely differentiable and equals its Taylor series.
primitives.computationalForm: Check Cauchy-Riemann equations: du/dx = dv/dy and du/dy = -dv/dx. If satisfied and partials are continuous, f is analytic. Alternatively, f'(z) = du/dx + i*dv/dx.

dependencies.description: Analyticity is a property of complex functions

compositionRules.yields: Cauchy theory: if f is analytic inside C, then oint_C f(z) dz = 0
conditions: f analytic in simply connected domain containing C
compositionRules.example: oint_{|z|=1} z^2 dz = 0 because z^2 is entire

applicabilityPatterns: Determine if a complex function is differentiable in the complex sense
Identify functions that have power series representations
Apply the powerful theorems of complex analysis to a function

primitives.name: Cauchy-Riemann Equations
primitives.formalStatement: A function f(z) = u(x,y) + iv(x,y) is analytic at z = x + iy if and only if (assuming continuous partials) the Cauchy-Riemann equations hold: du/dx = dv/dy and du/dy = -dv/dx.
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Write f(z) = u + iv. 2. Compute du/dx, du/dy, dv/dx, dv/dy. 3. Check: du/dx = dv/dy and du/dy = -dv/dx. If both hold (with continuous partials), f is analytic.

dependencies.description: CR equations characterize analyticity

compositionRules.yields: u and v are harmonic conjugates: nabla^2 u = 0 and nabla^2 v = 0 (Laplace equation)
compositionRules.example: f(z) = z^2: u = x^2-y^2, v = 2xy. Both satisfy Laplace's equation.

applicabilityPatterns: Test whether a complex function is analytic
Derive relationships between the real and imaginary parts of an analytic function
Show that real and imaginary parts of analytic functions are harmonic

primitives.name: Contour Integral
primitives.formalStatement: The contour integral of f along a curve C parametrized by z(t), a <= t <= b, is integral_C f(z) dz = integral_a^b f(z(t)) z'(t) dt. For a closed contour, write oint_C f(z) dz.
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Parametrize the contour: z(t) for t in [a,b]. 2. Compute z'(t). 3. Substitute: integral_a^b f(z(t)) * z'(t) dt. Common contours: circles z(t) = z_0 + re^{it}.

dependencies.description: Contour integrals are integrals of complex functions

compositionRules.yields: Evaluate integrals by summing residues: oint f(z) dz = 2*pi*i * sum of residues
conditions: C is a simple closed contour

applicabilityPatterns: Integrate a complex function along a path in the complex plane
Evaluate real integrals by extending to complex contours
Compute the integral of a function around a singularity

primitives.name: Cauchy's Integral Theorem
primitives.formalStatement: If f is analytic on and inside a simple closed contour C, then oint_C f(z) dz = 0. Equivalently, the integral of an analytic function depends only on the endpoints, not the path.
primitives.computationalForm: Check that f is analytic everywhere inside C (no singularities). If so, the contour integral is zero. If singularities exist inside C, use residue theorem instead.

dependencies.description: Theorem applies to analytic functions
Theorem is about contour integrals

compositionRules.yields: Evaluate f(z_0) from boundary data: f(z_0) = (1/2*pi*i) oint f(z)/(z-z_0) dz
compositionRules.example: f(z) = e^z, z_0 = 0, C = unit circle: (1/2*pi*i) oint e^z/z dz = e^0 = 1

applicabilityPatterns: Show that a contour integral of an analytic function is zero
Prove path independence for complex integrals
Deform contours through regions where f is analytic

primitives.name: Cauchy's Integral Formula
primitives.formalStatement: If f is analytic inside and on a simple closed contour C, and z_0 is inside C, then f(z_0) = (1/(2*pi*i)) oint_C f(z)/(z - z_0) dz. The n-th derivative: f^{(n)}(z_0) = (n!/(2*pi*i)) oint_C f(z)/(z - z_0)^{n+1} dz.
primitives.computationalForm: To find f(z_0): compute oint f(z)/(z-z_0) dz and divide by 2*pi*i. For derivatives: compute oint f(z)/(z-z_0)^{n+1} dz and multiply by n!/(2*pi*i).

dependencies.description: Integral formula is a consequence of Cauchy's theorem

compositionRules.yields: Coefficient extraction: a_n = (1/(2*pi*i)) oint f(z)/z^{n+1} dz (Laurent coefficients)
compositionRules.example: For e^z around z=0: a_n = 1/n! recovered from integral formula

applicabilityPatterns: Evaluate a complex function at a point using contour integration
Compute derivatives of analytic functions from integral representations
Prove that analytic functions are infinitely differentiable

primitives.name: Residue Theorem
primitives.formalStatement: If f is analytic inside and on a simple closed contour C except for isolated singularities z_1,...,z_n inside C, then oint_C f(z) dz = 2*pi*i * sum_{k=1}^{n} Res(f, z_k).
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Find all singularities inside C. 2. Compute residue at each: Res(f,z_k) = coefficient of 1/(z-z_k) in Laurent series. For simple poles: Res = lim_{z->z_k} (z-z_k)*f(z). 3. Sum and multiply by 2*pi*i.

dependencies.description: Residue theorem generalizes Cauchy's integral formula
Residues are Laurent series coefficients
Residue theorem applies at singularities

compositionRules.yields: Evaluation of real integrals via complex methods: integral_{-inf}^{inf} f(x) dx = 2*pi*i * sum of residues in upper half-plane
compositionRules.example: integral_{-inf}^{inf} 1/(1+x^2) dx = 2*pi*i * Res(1/(1+z^2), i) = 2*pi*i * 1/(2i) = pi

applicabilityPatterns: Evaluate complex contour integrals around singularities
Compute real definite integrals using complex analysis techniques
Sum series using residue methods

primitives.name: Laurent Series
primitives.formalStatement: The Laurent series of f about z_0 in an annular region R_1 < |z - z_0| < R_2 is f(z) = sum_{n=-inf}^{inf} a_n (z - z_0)^n where a_n = (1/(2*pi*i)) oint_C f(z)/(z-z_0)^{n+1} dz.
primitives.computationalForm: Expand f in positive and negative powers of (z - z_0). The principal part (negative powers) determines the singularity type. For computational use, partial fractions and known series expansions.

dependencies.description: Laurent series exists where f is analytic in an annulus
Coefficients defined via contour integrals

compositionRules.yields: Classification of singularities by Laurent series: removable (no negative terms), pole (finite negative terms), essential (infinite negative terms)
conditions: f has isolated singularity at z_0
compositionRules.example: 1/(z-1) has Laurent series with single term (z-1)^{-1}: simple pole

applicabilityPatterns: Expand a function with singularities as a series
Classify the type of an isolated singularity
Find the residue as the a_{-1} coefficient

primitives.name: Conformal Mapping
primitives.formalStatement: A conformal mapping is a function f: C -> C that preserves angles between curves at every point where f'(z) != 0. Analytic functions with nonzero derivative are conformal. Key examples: Mobius transformations f(z) = (az+b)/(cz+d), exponential maps.
primitives.computationalForm: Check conformality: verify f is analytic and f'(z) != 0. Mobius transformations: given three pairs (z_k, w_k), solve for a,b,c,d. Applications: map complicated domains to simple ones for easier integration.

dependencies.description: Conformal maps are analytic functions satisfying CR equations
Conformality requires analyticity

compositionRules.yields: Solve boundary value problems by mapping to simpler domains, integrating, mapping back
compositionRules.example: Map upper half-plane to unit disk via f(z) = (z-i)/(z+i), solve there, map back

applicabilityPatterns: Transform a complex domain into a simpler one for analysis
Preserve angles and local shape while remapping regions
Solve Laplace's equation by mapping to standard domains

primitives.name: Riemann Sphere
primitives.formalStatement: The Riemann sphere is the extended complex plane C union {infinity}, topologically equivalent to S^2 via stereographic projection. Mobius transformations act on the Riemann sphere as the group of conformal automorphisms.
primitives.computationalForm: Stereographic projection: point (X,Y,Z) on S^2 maps to z = (X+iY)/(1-Z). North pole maps to infinity. Arithmetic with infinity: z + inf = inf, z * inf = inf (for z != 0), z/0 = inf.

dependencies.description: Riemann sphere is the natural domain for Mobius transformations
Extends the complex plane by adding a point at infinity

compositionRules.yields: Complete theory of Mobius transformations on the Riemann sphere
compositionRules.example: f(z) = 1/z maps 0 to infinity and infinity to 0 on the Riemann sphere

applicabilityPatterns: Extend complex analysis to include infinity as a point
Visualize the complex plane as a sphere via stereographic projection
Classify all conformal maps of the sphere to itself

primitives.name: Maximum Modulus Principle
primitives.formalStatement: If f is analytic and non-constant on a connected open set D, then |f| has no local maximum in D. Consequently, if D is bounded and f is continuous on the closure of D, then |f| achieves its maximum on the boundary of D.
primitives.computationalForm: To find the maximum of |f| on a region: evaluate |f| only on the boundary. The maximum of |f| on the closure is attained on the boundary, never the interior (unless f is constant).

dependencies.description: Proof uses Cauchy's integral formula
Applies to analytic functions

compositionRules.yields: Rigidity of analytic functions: if |f| is constant on an open set, f is constant
conditions: f analytic on connected open set
compositionRules.example: If |e^z| = 1 on an open disk, then e^z is constant there (impossible for non-degenerate disk)

applicabilityPatterns: Find the maximum of the modulus of an analytic function
Prove bounds on analytic functions from boundary estimates
Show that analytic functions cannot have interior extrema for |f|

primitives.name: Poles and Singularities
primitives.formalStatement: An isolated singularity z_0 of f is: removable if lim_{z->z_0} f(z) exists finitely; a pole of order n if (z-z_0)^n * f(z) is analytic and nonzero at z_0; essential if neither. For a pole of order n, the residue is Res(f,z_0) = lim_{z->z_0} (1/(n-1)!) d^{n-1}/dz^{n-1} [(z-z_0)^n f(z)].
primitives.computationalForm: Classify: expand Laurent series. If no negative powers: removable. If finitely many negative powers up to (z-z_0)^{-n}: pole of order n. If infinitely many: essential. Residue at simple pole: Res = lim (z-z_0)*f(z).

dependencies.description: Singularity classification uses Laurent series
Singularities are points where analyticity fails

compositionRules.yields: Complete integration toolkit: classify singularities, compute residues, evaluate integrals
compositionRules.example: f(z) = 1/z^2 has pole of order 2 at z=0, Res = 0 (coefficient of 1/z is 0)

applicabilityPatterns: Classify the nature of a singularity of a complex function
Determine the order of a pole
Compute residues for use in the residue theorem
